titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to Lady Lillian Spender, 18 July 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968) In this letter, Spender goes into great detail describing the internal politics of the general staff with whom he works.Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
18 July My Pr Just back from a ride
on Fanny in which I went
wrong but quite enjoyed.
Sometimes I think she enjoys
a scamper with the bw as
as much as the pr. I won't say
which he best approves as
his companion ! No letter
last night but I did not expect
one on a Monday especially as
I was already one up. I have no news yet of Reg
but may hear something
about him at the base if
he does not come up at once.
Nor have I any news of my
going to the 24th which may not take place as we have
just changed our corps - for the
9th time, I believe; since we
came out. I don't know our
new staff at all but wish
we had gone to the next corps
where I know all of them. The Army Commander is
due here this morning, but
I don't expect I shall see
him, which is perhaps just
as well under the circumstance. Farnham has had a man
from his regiment asking
for his early return. I know
he much regrets leaving us
now, & his friendship with the GOC is a factor
which we shall greatly miss.
And his views were generally
sound on Ulster matters, & I
doubt if Sanderson will show
the same tact from what I have
heard of him
Gen' Powell & Sir G both
wrote to the G asking him to
put their congratulations in
orders - the former several pages! I must say I sympathize
with the G in this, & I think
he is right in declining. It is a
little lacking in tact like the
publication of his & my letters
without authority. Sir G also
published a UVF order with the
G's & the former, who is very
bitter at the desertion of his
county - not unnaturally - &
who wishes he had never
touched the UVF now is quite
furious. I think he is justified
& that Sir G ought to have
realize this. I am writing a
line of caution to Sir G. Col
J.C would never have made
these mistakes. We get news
papers the same evening as
they are published now, & letters
come with them but my
arrows are likely to be very
much delayed if I go to the
24 Div to help. I know their
GSO2 & A man quite well
we are old pals so that it
won't be quite 'going among
strangers' not as if I were
leaving the Division altogether
as I expect it will be only for
3 weeks when the Div may
join me again. Still even
this is unpleasant as one's
position away from one's
own men is quite different,
as you will understand. I don't like to hear of your
long office hours. Please feed
yourself well. You will need it
with this extra work now on. Your bw WB Spender
